topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark intrusion into the quiet rhythm of my evening. My breath hitched, a small, involuntary catch in my chest. It was a profile picture I hadn't seen in over a decade—a grainy photo taken at some poorly lit summer camp, featuring a grin that used to make my stomach clench. A message followed: "Hey. Long time no talk." My fingers hovered over the 'Accept' button, the plastic cool beneath my touch. A strange tightening began in my shoulders, a subtle bracing against an unseen pressure. I stared at the screen, tracing the outline of their name, a slow, deliberate movement that felt heavy with unspoken history. I quickly minimized the window, the sound of the notification chime seeming deafening in the silence of my apartment.

[story 2]

I glanced at my phone as if it were a live wire. The request was from Mark. Mark, the architect of my awkward middle school years, the one whose shadow always seemed to precede me down the hallway. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscle twitching in my neck. When I finally opened the chat, the opening line was casual, almost breezy. "What's up?" I typed back, my thumbs moving slowly, deliberately. A wave of heat rushed up my neck, making the fine hairs on my arms prickle. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest. I kept glancing towards the window, expecting something to materialize in the street, a familiar shape moving too quickly. Every keystroke felt weighted, a decision made under duress.

[story 3]

The email landed in my inbox with an abruptness that made me jump. It was from David. The name alone was enough to send a cold tremor through my limbs. I reread the message three times, the words echoing hollowly in the sterile light of my desk lamp. There was no preamble, just a simple invitation to connect. I gripped the edge of the wooden desk until my knuckles were white, the wood digging painfully into my palms. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out the ambient sounds of the city. I didn't know whether to delete it immediately or to let it sit there, a burning object demanding attention. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action difficult.
